text: The surgeon performs laparoscopic rectopexy for complete rectal prolapse, with ports placed and dissection of the retrorectal space underway. The uterus is visible and retracted anteriorly using a percutaneous stitch.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic rectopexy for complete rectal prolapse, with ports placed and dissection of the retrorectal space underway. The uterus is visible and retracted anteriorly using a percutaneous stitch.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to the rectum and places a polypropylene mesh over the posterior two-thirds of the rectum, leaving the anterior one-third uncovered for expansion. The mesh is fixed to the sacral promontory using intracorporeal sutures with 1-0 Prolene, while carefully avoiding the median sacral vessels.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to the rectum, revealing a polypropylene mesh covering the posterior two-thirds of the rectum while leaving the anterior one-third uncovered to allow for rectal expansion.
The surgeon fixes the polypropylene mesh to the sacral promontory using intracorporeal sutures, specifically one-zero proline sutures, while carefully avoiding the median sacral vessels.

The surgeon sutures the lateral end of the polypropylene mesh to the rectum, ensuring secure attachment. The middle rectal ligament is visible, and its division is considered to allow further cranial traction of the rectum.

The surgeon sutures the lateral end of the polypropylene mesh to the rectum, ensuring not to take a through-and-through bite to avoid infection. The middle rectal ligament is visible, with consideration given to whether dividing it would allow further cranial traction.
The surgeon has wrapped the mesh around the rectum on both sides, leaving the anterior part of the rectum uncovered to allow for expansion.

The surgeon wraps the mesh around the rectum, leaving the anterior part uncovered. To prevent intestinal herniation or adhesions to the mesh, the peritoneal flap is closed on either side using continuous 2-0 Vicryl sutures.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap on either side using continuous 2-0 Vicryl sutures to prevent intestinal herniation or adhesions to the mesh.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to the rectum while fixing the mesh to its lateral aspect. The peritoneal defect on the left side is closed, and the closure will be similarly performed on the right side.
